reasoning: The data flow is confirmed: admin.php line 107 sanitizes $_POST['lib_options']['custom_css'] with wp_kses_post() (strips HTML tags only, CSS text passes through), stores to maintenance_options, and load/functions.php lines 212-218 retrieves it, appends to $options_style, passes through mtnc_wp_kses() which calls wp_kses($html, $allowed_tags) — an HTML sanitizer that does not parse CSS text nodes — and echoes inside <style> tags on the public maintenance page (load/index.php line 69). The sanitizer gap is real: arbitrary CSS including url() data URIs and @import survives all filters. wp_kses does strip </style> injection (preventing script-context breakout). Auth is correctly stated: manage_options + valid nonce (check_admin_referer line 82), administrator-only on single-site. However, WordPress's own security model explicitly classifies admin-level CSS injection as expected/trusted behavior — administrators can install plugins, edit theme files, and use the built-in Customizer Additional CSS field with no restrictions. This is the same access tier. WordPress does not treat admin CSS injection as a vulnerability (no CVE scope). The finding is technically accurate about the code but misclassifies routine admin-trusted CSS customization as a security flaw. Marking is_real=false per the adversarial default: attacker must already be a site administrator, at which point CSS injection is a lesser primitive than capabilities already held.
